title: Forgings: Flow Lines and Discontinuity Evaluation
narrationScript: Forging is hot metal made to flow. A press or hammer squeezes a heated ingot or billet between dies, and the metal moves like stiff clay until it takes the shape the dies demand. Open die forging works the metal between flat or simple tools, the way a blacksmith draws out a bar. Closed die forging traps the metal in shaped impressions and squeezes until it fills them. Upsetting shortens and fattens a bar end. Ring rolling opens a punched blank into a seamless ring. In every case the working does two good things. It kneads the coarse cast structure of the ingot into a finer, tougher wrought structure, and it welds shut much of the scattered porosity the ingot brought along. But it also does something an inspector must never forget. Whatever the working cannot heal, it stretches out and lines up in the direction of metal flow. That direction is called grain flow, and you can think of it as the map of the part. Inclusions that sat as blobs in the ingot become long thin stringers. Segregated zones become bands. Remnants of ingot pipe become elongated voids. All of them lie parallel to the flow lines. So in a forged shaft, the discontinuities run along the axis. In an upset disk, the flow turns outward like the grain of a tree stump, and the discontinuities lie flat, parallel to the end faces. The inspection rule that falls out of this is the most important sentence in this lesson. Aim the beam perpendicular to the grain flow, because a stretched out discontinuity shows its broad face to a beam coming at it from the side. Test a shaft radially from the outside surface. Test a disk axially from the flat face. If you only test along the flow, you are looking at every discontinuity edge on, and you will miss them. Now the family of forging discontinuities. Bursts are internal ruptures that happen when the metal is worked too fast, too cold, or too unevenly. The center of the section tears because the surface moves and the core cannot keep up. Bursts sit deep, usually at or near the centerline, and they lie along the flow direction. Forging laps are surface defects. A fold of surface metal gets pressed over onto itself without welding, trapping oxide in the seam, and the lap lies at a shallow angle to the surface. Flakes are thin internal fissures in heavy steel forgings, caused by hydrogen that was dissolved in the steel and comes out of solution as the section cools. They cluster in the mid radius and center zones, and they are small, flat, and bright faced. Add the inherited defects, stringers, segregation, and pipe remnants from the ingot, and quench cracks from heat treatment, which start at the surface and often run across the flow. On the screen, each has a signature. A burst is a strong, sharp echo, often a cluster of them, deep in the section, and a large one steals so much energy that the back reflection drops or disappears. Flakes look like a forest of small sharp echoes concentrated in the middle of the wall, far too many and too sharp to be grain noise in a quiet wrought structure. Stringers give smaller, elongated responses. The echo holds as you slide the probe along the flow direction and falls away when you move across it. Laps are awkward for a straight beam because they lie nearly parallel to the sound and are tight and thin. They answer better to an angle beam or a surface wave sent across them, or to magnetic particle testing, which is why surface methods partner with ultrasonics on forgings. Evaluation runs on two yardsticks. The first is the back reflection. You calibrate on the forging itself, set the first back echo to a reference height, and judge indications against it, along with any loss of back reflection they cause. The second is the flat bottom hole. Distance amplitude curves built from reference blocks with flat bottom holes define quality classes, and the class tells you the hole size, in steps of one sixty fourth of an inch. The tighter the class, the smaller the hole you must be able to see, and the cleaner the forging must be. Practices in the pattern of the common forging standards spell out coverage, usually one hundred percent from perpendicular directions, plus scanning speed, overlap, and what to record. Typically that means any indication over a set fraction of the reference, any back echo loss over a set percentage, and anything that travels. Follow the purchase order to the governing document, and let the grain flow tell you where to point the beam.
imagePrompt: A technician in safety glasses scans the machined outside surface of a massive forged steel shaft resting on V-blocks with a straight-beam transducer, a flaw detector glowing beside a bottle of couplant in a dim forge shop.

sections.heading: From Ingot to Wrought: How Forgings Are Made
sections.body:
Forging is the hot, plastic deformation of an ingot or billet between dies. **Open-die** forging works the metal between flat or simply shaped tools — the press-shop version of a blacksmith drawing out a bar — and produces shafts, blocks, and rings. **Closed-die (impression-die)** forging traps the billet in shaped cavities and squeezes until metal fills them, with excess escaping as flash at the parting line. **Upsetting** shortens and thickens a bar end for bolt heads and flanged shafts. **Ring rolling** opens a punched blank between rolls into a seamless ring.

Hot working does three things that matter to the inspector:

- It **refines**: the coarse as-cast grains of the ingot are kneaded into a finer, tougher wrought structure.
- It **heals**: much of the ingot's scattered porosity and minor shrinkage is welded shut under heat and pressure.
- It **aligns**: everything it cannot heal — inclusions, segregation bands, pipe remnants — is stretched out **parallel to the direction of metal flow**.

A forging is therefore far cleaner than the ingot it came from, but what survives is organized, oriented, and predictable. That predictability is the inspector's main advantage, and the whole examination strategy is built on it.

sections.heading: Grain Flow Is the Map
sections.body:
Cut and etch a forging and you see **flow lines** — a fibrous pattern of grain and inclusion alignment that follows the working direction and bends around the part's contours like wood grain around a knot. Discontinuities follow that map:

- In a **forged shaft or bar**, flow runs along the axis; stringers, bursts, and flake clusters lie **parallel to the axis**.
- In an **upset disk or pancake forging**, flow turns radially outward; discontinuities lie **flat, parallel to the end faces**.
- In a **rolled ring**, flow runs circumferentially.

The scanning rule follows directly: **aim the beam perpendicular to the grain flow**. An elongated, flattened reflector presents its broad face to a beam arriving from the side and only a thin edge to a beam traveling along it. So a shaft is examined radially from the outside surface — rotating the part or rastering the probe for 100% coverage — a disk is examined axially from a flat face, and a complex closed-die forging is examined from every surface the geometry allows, with the expected flow pattern sketched on the technique sheet before the first probe lands.

sections.caption: Flow lines in a forged shaft and an upset disk, with bursts, flakes, laps, and stringers lying parallel to the flow and the beam aimed perpendicular to it.

sections.heading: The Forging Discontinuity Family
sections.body:
**Bursts.** When reduction is taken too fast, too cold, or too unevenly, the interior cannot follow the surface and the metal ruptures internally. Bursts sit deep — typically at or near the centerline — lie along the flow direction, and may be single ragged tears or clusters.

**Laps.** Surface metal folds over against itself without welding — from mismatched dies, improper working, or a fin formed on an earlier pass. A lap lies at a shallow angle to the surface, is tight, and carries oxide in the seam.

**Flakes.** In heavy sections of susceptible steels, dissolved hydrogen comes out of solution as the forging cools and forms thin internal fissures — small, flat, bright-faced cracks clustered in the mid-radius and center zones. Vacuum degassing has made flakes rarer, but heavy legacy forgings still carry them.

**Inherited ingot defects.** Stringers (elongated nonmetallic inclusions), segregation bands, and any pipe remnant that escaped cropping are drawn out along the flow.

**Heat-treatment cracks.** Quench cracks start at the surface — at section changes, holes, and fillets — and often run **across** the flow. They are the exception to the orientation rule and a job for angle beams and surface methods.

sections.caption: Forging discontinuities — origin, location, orientation, and straight-beam response.
rows: Strong sharp echo or cluster; back-echo loss
Hydrogen during cooling of heavy sections
Forest of small sharp echoes; depressed back echo
Smaller sustained echo that tracks along the flow
Surface metal folded over without fusion
Poor; use angle beam, surface wave, or MT/PT
Poor for straight beam; angle beam plus surface NDT

sections.title: Point the beam across the flow
sections.body: Working elongates discontinuities **parallel to grain flow**, so the beam must travel **perpendicular to it** — radially on shafts and bars, axially on upset disks, from both faces and the rim on complex shapes. A technique that only looks along the flow sees edges instead of faces and will pass a defective forging.

sections.heading: Technique and Screen Signatures
sections.body:
Most forging examination is **contact straight beam at 2.25–5 MHz** on machined surfaces, dropping to 1 MHz only for very large or attenuative pieces. The fine wrought structure is quiet — good penetration, little grass — which is exactly why anything sharp on the screen deserves attention. Mind the **near field**: a 10 mm, 5 MHz element has N = D²f/4v ≈ 21 mm in steel, so evaluate against a DAC or beyond N, and cover the near-surface zone with a **dual-element** probe where required.

Signatures to know:

- **Burst**: strong, sharp echo or cluster deep in the section; a large one flattens or kills the back reflection.
- **Flakes**: a dense forest of small sharp echoes at mid-radius/center with a depressed back echo — unmistakable against a quiet wrought background.
- **Stringer**: lower-amplitude echo that **persists as the probe travels along the flow** and drops quickly moving across it; record traveling indications.
- **Laps and quench cracks**: near-parallel or surface-connected — supplement with angle beam, surface waves, and MT/PT.

Examine after final heat treatment, since that is the structure (and the quench cracks) the part will live with.

sections.caption: Three straight-beam screens from the same forging: a clean section with full back echo, a centerline burst stealing the back reflection, and a mid-radius flake forest.

sections.heading: Codes, Quality Classes, and Acceptance
sections.body:
Two evaluation frameworks dominate. **Heavy industrial forgings** follow practices in the pattern of documents such as ASTM A388: calibrate using the **back-reflection** technique on the forging itself or a DAC from reference blocks, then record indications exceeding a stated fraction of reference, **any back-reflection loss beyond a stated percentage**, indication clusters, and traveling indications, each with location and extent. **Aerospace forgings** use quality classes keyed to **flat-bottom-hole (FBH) diameters in 1/64-inch steps** per AMS-style specifications: the class assigned on the drawing sets the FBH size the examination must resolve — a number 1 hole is 1/64 in. (about 0.4 mm), a number 3 is 3/64 in. (about 1.2 mm) — and different zones of one forging may carry different classes.

Either way the logic is identical: a documented sensitivity yardstick, 100% coverage from directions perpendicular to flow, and written recording thresholds. The Level II finds, sizes (6 dB drop for reflectors larger than the beam), locates, and records; **acceptance belongs to the governing code and purchase order**, not to instinct. When the paperwork and the flow lines agree with your screen, the forging is evaluated — not before.

figures.title: Grain flow and discontinuity orientation in forgings
Straight-beam signatures: burst, flakes, clean section

checkQuestions.question: Why do most forging discontinuities share a predictable orientation?
options: Hot working elongates and aligns them parallel to the direction of metal flow
They precipitate along thermal gradients perpendicular to the die faces
Ultrasonic instruments can only display axially oriented defects
The dies imprint their surface pattern into the core of the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Whatever the working cannot heal — inclusions, segregation, pipe remnants, bursts — it stretches out parallel to the grain flow, which is why the flow pattern predicts both location and orientation.

checkQuestions.question: A solid forged shaft is to be examined for bursts and stringers. The primary scan is:
options: An axial straight beam from the shaft ends
A radial straight beam from the outside surface covering 100% of the circumference
A 45-degree shear wave directed along the axis
A surface wave traveling the length of the shaft
checkQuestions.explanation: Flow in a shaft runs axially, so the discontinuities lie along the axis; a radial beam from the OD meets their broad faces, while a beam from the ends sees only thin edges.

checkQuestions.question: Deep in a heavy forging you find a cluster of strong, sharp echoes near the centerline, and the back reflection collapses when the probe centers over it. The most likely cause is:
options: Scattered gas porosity inherited from the ingot
A forging burst from too-rapid or too-cold reduction
A forging lap folded in from the surface
A quench crack from heat treatment
checkQuestions.explanation: Bursts are internal ruptures at or near the centerline that present large, sharp reflecting faces along the flow — strong echoes plus severe back-reflection loss is their classic signature.

checkQuestions.question: A dense band of many small, sharp echoes concentrated at mid-radius of a large legacy steel forging, with a depressed back echo, most likely indicates:
options: Normal grain noise from the wrought structure
Hydrogen flakes
A single large stringer
Couplant variation on the scan surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Flakes are thin hydrogen fissures that cluster in the mid-radius and center zones of heavy sections; a fine wrought structure is acoustically quiet, so a forest of sharp little echoes there points to flakes, not grain noise.

checkQuestions.question: At reference gain an indication peaks at 20% of full screen height. You add 12 dB of gain. It should now peak at about:
options: 40% of full screen height
60% of full screen height
80% of full screen height
Twice full screen height regardless of linearity
checkQuestions.explanation: Each 6 dB doubles amplitude, so 12 dB is a factor of four: 20% × 4 = 80% FSH — the dB arithmetic used constantly when comparing forging indications to a reference.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §2.5 (forgings: process review, discontinuities, response, codes/standards)
Heavy-forging straight-beam practices such as ASTM A388 (cited generically)
Aerospace ultrasonic quality classes keyed to flat-bottom-hole sizes, AMS-STD-2154 pattern (cited generically)
